title: Update the top-level library documentation to help new users
body:
We want to encourage developers to extend library transport types.

**User story**
As a developer who wants to extend the library to integrate with my 3rd party tech, I want some documentation for how to do this.

comments.body:
`s/signature transport/image transport/` in the title?

The short version is “implement a `types.ImageTransport` and add it to `containers/image/transports.KnownTransports`”; we’ve been trying fairly hard to document `types.go` extensively. I realize that’s not really a step-by-step guide, but given this, what are the main missing pieces? I suppose placing the quoted sentence somewhere, at the very least; other than that?

> s/signature transport/image transport/ in the title?

Yes. Updated

> The short version is “implement a types.ImageTransport and add it to containers/image/transports.KnownTransports”; we’ve been trying fairly hard to document types.go extensively.

Inline documentation is very good. What's missing is a high-level doc to provide some guidance what this library can do generally, interesting extension and integration points.
@aweiteka  @mtrmac @vrothberg What is the state of this issue. It is two years old, can we close it?
I think we can close it. The documentation, including man pages, has been improved quite a lot over time.

No “high-level doc to provide some guidance what this library can do generally, interesting extension and integration points” exists; the full extent of our guidance is https://github.com/containers/image/blob/master/doc.go and a single mention of `copy.Image` in `README.md`.

We don’t necessarily need a new document, but improving the top-level GoDoc package description on `c/image/doc.go` still seems useful.

Meanwhile, we have made a lot of the transport interfaces private, so that we can add features without breaking externally-observable API.

And there has been just one new transport added to c/image during that time (but we might not be aware of any external ones).

Third-party providers can also only work for third-party binaries that link their own copy of c/image; there is no plugin mechanism to add a transport to a pre-built, supported, Podman binary.

Overall, I think the balance has clearly shifted enough towards encouraging new transports to be contributed to this repo, not encouraging third-party external providers to be maintained separately.

---

Still, the top-level `doc.go` could definitely be much improved. So let’s track that part.
